g.data-narration: Welcome to the Introduction to Data Structures module. This topic covers Stacks and Queues, fundamental linear data structures essential for algorithm design and problem-solving in computer science.
A data structure is a specialized way of organizing and storing data in computer memory for efficient access and modification. It determines how data is managed within a computer system.
g.text: A specialized way of organizing and storing data in computer memory for efficient
access and modification. Key to efficient algorithm design and implementation.

g.data-narration: An Abstract Data Type (ADT) defines a set of operations without specifying implementation details. It separates the interface from how operations are actually performed in code.
g.text: Defines operations (push, pop, enqueue, dequeue) without specifying implementation.
Separates interface from implementation, enabling code maintainability and flexibility.

g.data-narration: Linear data structures arrange elements sequentially where each element has one predecessor and one successor. Arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues are all linear structures.
g.text: Elements arranged sequentially; each has one predecessor and one successor.
Distinguished by how elements are added and removed (access patterns).

g.data-narration: A Stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) principle. Think of a plate stack - only top plate can be added or removed. Operations: push, pop, and peek.
g.text: Last element added is first removed. Operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top).
Applications: function calls, undo mechanisms, expression evaluation, backtracking.

g.data-narration: A Queue follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) principle. Like a ticket line - first person arrives is served first. Operations: enqueue (add rear) and dequeue (remove front).
g.text: First element added is first removed. Enqueue adds to rear, dequeue removes from front.
Applications: CPU scheduling, print spooling, BFS algorithms, request handling.

g.data-narration: Remember: Stack uses LIFO, Queue uses FIFO. Time complexity for push/pop and enqueue/dequeue is O(1). Check for underflow before operations.
g.text: • Stack = LIFO | Queue = FIFO (frequently tested in exams)
• Time Complexity: push/pop and enqueue/dequeue are O(1) - constant time
• Always check for underflow (empty) before pop/dequeue to avoid runtime errors
